explanation: This is a safety property. If at any time prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same time. So, globally, whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true: G(prop_0 -> prop_1).

The statement says that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time or immediately after. In LTL, this is typically expressed globally as 'whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 is true at that point.'
This expresses that at all times, if 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1' must also hold at that same moment. In LTL, this is written as 'G (prop_0 -> prop_1)'.

This statement requires that whenever prop_0 holds at a position, then prop_1 must also hold at the same position. In LTL, this is a safety property best captured as 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1.'

This statement expresses that whenever 'prop_0' holds, 'prop_1' must also hold, at all times. In LTL, this is best expressed using globally (G): 'It is always the case that if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds.'
The requirement 'If prop_0, then prop_1.' means that at all times, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. This is a safety property expressed globally.
The specification expresses that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same instant. This is globally enforced using the implication operator.
This expresses that globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time.

This expresses that globally, whenever 'prop_0' holds, 'prop_1' must also hold at that moment. This is a classic form of implication in LTL: if 'prop_0' becomes true at any point, 'prop_1' must also be true then.
This formula captures that globally (at all times), if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same time. This is a classic safety property.
